questions.question: What is the learning curve for implementing CAD/CAM in our practice?
questions.answer: Most dental teams become comfortable with the basic workflow within 2-3 weeks. Our implementation includes comprehensive hands-on training for your entire team. The scanning process typically has the shortest learning curve (1-2 days), while design skills develop over a few weeks of regular use. We provide ongoing support with virtual check-ins during your first month, and our clinical education team is available for advanced training as your skills progress.

questions.question: What types of materials can be used with the system?
questions.answer: Our milling units are compatible with a wide range of materials including lithium disilicate (e.max), zirconia, hybrid ceramics (Enamic), composites, PMMA, and wax. The Professional and Enterprise systems can also mill titanium pre-milled abutments. All materials come in various shades and translucencies to match any clinical situation, and multi-layered blocks are available for enhanced esthetics in the anterior region.

questions.question: What is the return on investment timeline?
questions.answer: Most practices achieve ROI within 18-24 months. With an average lab bill reduction of 60% and the ability to complete restorations in a single visit, practices typically see a positive cash flow impact immediately. Practices completing just 15-20 restorations per month will fully cover their monthly equipment cost, with additional units representing pure profit compared to traditional lab-based workflows.

questions.question: Can we still work with our lab for complex cases?
questions.answer: Absolutely. Our open architecture system allows you to easily export scan files in standard STL format, which can be sent to any dental lab. This creates a hybrid workflow where routine cases are handled in-house while complex cases are sent to your lab. Many practices handle 80-90% of their restorative cases in-house, maintaining lab relationships for complex full-arch cases, removable prosthetics, or esthetically demanding anterior cases.

questions.question: How accurate are the digital impressions compared to traditional methods?
questions.answer: Multiple independent studies have shown that digital impressions are equal or superior to traditional impressions in accuracy. Our scanner achieves a precision of under 10 microns, which exceeds the capabilities of physical impression materials. Additionally, digital impressions eliminate many sources of error in traditional methods, including impression material distortion, disinfection issues, and model pouring inaccuracies. The digital process also allows immediate verification and selective rescanning if needed.

reviews.name: Dr. Michael Anderson
reviews.comment: Implementing CAD/CAM technology has been transformative for my practice. We've reduced lab costs by over 70% while delivering higher quality restorations. Patients love the single-visit convenience, and my team enjoys the predictable digital workflow. The system paid for itself in just 14 months, and we're now seeing significantly higher profit margins on all restorative procedures.

reviews.name: Dr. Jennifer Patel
reviews.comment: The precision of the restorations has exceeded my expectations. Our marginal fit is consistently excellent, and the esthetic outcomes rival what we previously achieved with our high-end lab. The digital workflow has eliminated remakes almost entirely, and our patients are amazed by the technology. We're attracting new patients specifically because we offer same-day restorations.

reviews.name: Dr. Robert Thompson
reviews.comment: As someone who was initially skeptical about in-office milling, I've been completely won over by the quality and efficiency. The learning curve was much easier than I anticipated, and the support team helped us integrate the technology smoothly. We're now completing 40-50 restorations monthly in-house, and our production has increased by nearly 30% with the same staff hours.
